# Bayes' theorem with discrete models

Bayesian inference is just bookkeeping with probability. We start with what we
believe *before* seeing data (the **prior**), multiply by how well each
hypothesis explains the data (the **likelihood**), and renormalise to get what
we believe *after* (the **posterior**).

## A marine example

An acoustic tag pings and is picked up by receivers, but the detection pattern
is ambiguous: the fish could have come from the **North**, **Mid**, or **South**
reef. From past tracking we know how likely that detection pattern is from each
reef — these are the **likelihoods**.

## The theorem, term by term

$$
\underbrace{P(\text{reef} \mid \text{data})}_{\text{posterior}}
=
\frac{
  \overbrace{P(\text{data} \mid \text{reef})}^{\text{likelihood}}\;
  \overbrace{P(\text{reef})}^{\text{prior}}
}{
  \underbrace{\sum_{r} P(\text{data} \mid \text{reef}_r)\,P(\text{reef}_r)}_{\text{denominator}}
}
$$

- The **numerator** for each reef is just `prior × likelihood`.
- The **denominator** is *the sum of the numerators across all reefs*. It is the
  total probability of the data, and it is what makes the posterior add to 1.

That second point is the key idea: **the denominator is not a separate mystery
quantity — it is built from the same numerators you already computed.**

## Your task

In the challenge below, fill in the numerator for each reef, then the
denominator (the sum), then each posterior. Then switch the prior and watch the
posterior change — same data, different starting beliefs.
